title: torch.compile(dynamic=True) specializes batch dimension for LayerNorm backward, causing recompiles
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamic shapes
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug **Summary:** When using `make_fx` with `tracing_mode='symbolic'` to trace a module containing `nn.LayerNorm` and `torch.autograd.grad(create_graph=True)`, and then compiling the resulting FX graph with `torch.compile(dynamic=True, fullgraph=True)`, the batch dimension (`dim 0`) gets specialized. This causes a new recompilation for every new batch size, triggered by a "guard size mismatch at index 0". This behavior is specific to the presence of the `native_layer_norm_backward` operator in the graph. A forward-only `LayerNorm` graph compiles with a truly dynamic batch dimension as expected. **Background:** I am working on a machine learning force field project and aim to accelerate my model's training process using torch.compile. Briefly, the machine learning force field model reads distance vectors between atoms and outputs energy and forces, where the latter are derived by computing the gradient of energy with respect to the distance vectors. In other words, the forward() function of my model contains manually invoked autograd operations. Additionally, the loss function of my model consists of two components (energy and forces), which means that higher-order gradients need to be computed during the backward pass of training. Meanwhile, the "batch_size" varies for each batch (since the number of atoms in each molecule is different), which requires that the code generated by compilation does not statically fix the dimensions of the input tensors. To address this, I first traced my computation graph using make_fx(mode="symbolic"), then compiled the model with torch.compile(dynamic=True). However, I found that the compiled output still has statically fixed dimensions, leading to frequent recompilation when feeding inputs of different shapes. After analysis, I identified that the root cause of the recompilation is the backward operator of torch.nn.LayerNorm. A minimal reproducible example is provided below: **Minimal Reproducer:** ```python import os import torch import torch.nn as nn from torch.fx import make_fx # Set logs to observe guard failures and recompilations os.environ["TORCH_LOGS"] = "dynamic,guards,recompiles" class TinyDemo(nn.Module): """ A simple model that applies LayerNorm and computes its gradient w.r.t. input. """ def __init__(self, dim=64): super().__init__() self.dim = dim self.layer_norm = nn.LayerNorm(self.dim) def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor): x.requires_grad_(True) y = self.layer_norm(x) grad_outputs = [torch.ones_like(y)] # create_graph=True is essential to produce native_layer_norm_backward in the graph grad = torch.autograd.grad( [y], [x], grad_outputs=grad_outputs, create_graph=True, allow_unused=True )[0] return y, grad # 1. Trace the model with symbolic tracing model = TinyDemo(dim=64) example_input = torch.randn(10, 64) fx_model = make_fx( model, tracing_mode='symbolic', _allow_non_fake_inputs=True, _error_on_data_dependent_ops=True, )(example_input) print("--- FX Graph Code ---") print(fx_model.code) # 2. Compile the FX graph with dynamic=True compiled_model = torch.compile( fx_model, dynamic=True, fullgraph=True ) # 3. Run with varying batch sizes to trigger recompilations print("\\n--- Running with different batch sizes ---") for n in [10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90]: print(f"Running with batch size N={n}") x = torch.randn(n, 64) try: y, g = compiled_model(x) print(f" Success: y.shape={y.shape}, g.shape={g.shape}") except Exception as e: print(f" Failed with error: {e}") print("\\n--- End of script ---") ``` [tl_out.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/24736313/tl_out.zip) ### Error logs **Expected Behavior:** With `torch.compile(dynamic=True)`, the compiled model should handle a dynamic batch dimension (`N`) without recompiling. The first run (e.g., `N=10`) should produce a compiled artifact where the batch dimension is treated as a `SymInt`. Subsequent runs with different `N` values (20, 30, etc.) should reuse this artifact without triggering any guard failures related to the batch dimension. **Actual Behavior:** For each new batch size `N`, a recompilation is triggered. The logs show a series of guard failures indicating that the batch dimension was specialized to the previous value. For example, when running with `N=90` after running with `N=10` through `N=80`, the logs show: > V0120... [__recompiles] Recompiling function forward... > V0120... [__recompiles] triggered by the following guard failure(s): > V0120... [__recompiles] - 0/7: tensor 'arg0_1' size mismatch at index 0. expected 80, actual 90 > V0120... [__recompiles] - 0/6: tensor 'arg0_1' size mismatch at index 0. expected 70, actual 90 > ... > V0120... [__recompiles] - 0/0: tensor 'arg0_1' size mismatch at index 0. expected 10, actual 90 ### Versions PyTorch version: 2.9.1 Is debug build: False CUDA used to build PyTorch: 13.0 ROCM used to build PyTorch: N/A OS: Debian GNU/Linux 12 (bookworm) (x86_64) GCC version: (Debian 12.2.0-14+deb12u1) 12.2.0 Clang version: Could not collect CMake version: version 3.31.6 Libc version: glibc-2.36 Python version: 3.12.4 (main, Dec 15 2025, 11:08:09) [GCC 12.2.0] (64-bit runtime) Python platform: Linux-5.4.210.bsk.6-amd64-x86_64-with-glibc2.36 Is CUDA available: False CUDA runtime version: 13.0.88 Nvidia driver version: Could not collect cuDNN version: Probably one of the following: /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libcudnn.so.9.16.0 /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libcudnn_adv.so.9.16.0 /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libcudnn_cnn.so.9.16.0 /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libcudnn_engines_precompiled.so.9.16.0 /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libcudnn_engines_runtime_compiled.so.9.16.0 /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libcudnn_graph.so.9.16.0 /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libcudnn_heuristic.so.9.16.0 /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libcudnn_ops.so.9.16.0 Is XPU available: False HIP runtime version: N/A MIOpen runtime version: N/A Is XNNPACK available: True Caching allocator config: N/A CPU: Architecture: x86_64 CPU op-mode(s): 32-bit, 64-bit Address sizes: 46 bits physical, 57 bits virtual Byte title: mega cache does not contain triton cubin files
labels: needs reproduction, module: docs, triaged, needs research, oncall: pt2, compile-cache
body: ### 📚 The doc issue As written now the docs here https://docs.pytorch.org/tutorials/recipes/torch_compile_caching_tutorial.html make it seem like `torch.compiler.save_cache_artifacts()` and its load counterpart, will mean you carry around the triton code, and cubin files (after setting inductor_config.triton.store_cubin = True` of course. This is not the case, reloading the cache gives you no triton artefacts. I had to write my own cacheartifactmanager stuff to get this to happen. ### Suggest a potential alternative/fix Either fix the docs, or actually save the triton artefacts cc @svekars @sekyondaMeta @AlannaBurke @chauhang @penguinwu @oulgen @jamesjwu @masnesral

title: Cold start models with compile-cache have significant overhead
labels: needs reproduction, triaged, oncall: pt2
body: Cold start models with compile-cache still incur substantial overhead, even when caching is enabled. This occurs with both TORCHINDUCTOR_CACHE_DIR and torch.compiler.save_cache_artifacts. I’ve attached two minimal reproducible examples: - using `TORCHINDUCTOR_CACHE_DIR`: `test_cache.py` - using `torch.compiler.save_cache_artifacts`: `test_cache_load.py` [test_cache_load.py](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/24644135/test_cache_load.py) [test_cache.py](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/24644141/test_cache.py) For both experiments, the following environment variables are set: ``` export TORCHINDUCTOR_FX_GRAPH_CACHE=1 export TORCHINDUCTOR_AUTOGRAD_CACHE=1 ``` Results Using torch==2.9.1+cu126 on a H100 and test_cache_load.py: - First iteration without compilation: 0.25 s - First iteration with compilation: 3.36 s - First iteration with compilation + cache loading: 1.03 s Question It’s unclear whether this behavior is expected. The documentation notes that caching reduces compilation time but does not quantify the remaining overhead. - Is this level of cold-start overhead expected? - If so, what additional steps are recommended to further reduce it? to reproduce the above results run ``` export TORCH_LOGS=+torch._inductor.codecache export TORCHINDUCTOR_CACHE_DIR=$(pwd)/cache export TORCHINDUCTOR_FX_GRAPH_CACHE=1 export TORCHINDUCTOR_AUTOGRAD_CACHE=1 rm -rf $(pwd)/cache rm compile_artifact.obj python test_cache_load.py --compile &> compile.txt python test_cache_load.py --compile &> cached_compile.txt python test_cache_load.py &> no_compile.txt ``` cc @chauhang @penguinwu

title: [dynamo] Include variable names in recompilation error messages due to specialization on cell objects
labels: oncall: pt2, module: dynamo
body: ### 🚀 The feature, motivation and pitch It may be possible to recover a variable name corresponding to the specialized objects in the closures. That would help pinpoint which object was specialized on. ```python [ "1/6: ___check_obj_id(block_mask.mask_mod.__closure__[1].cell_contents.__closure__[0].cell_contents[0].__code__, 140406789936816), type=<code object fn at 0x7fb300d696b0, file \\".../attention_mask.py\\", line 687> # result = mask_mods[0](b_idx, h_idx, q_idx, k_idx) # .../attention_mask.py:688 in fn", "1/5: ___check_type_id(block_mask.mask_mod.__closure__[0].cell_contents, 93981787756512), type=<class 'tuple'> # result = mask_mods[0](b_idx, h_idx, q_idx, k_idx) # ...attention_mask.py:688 in fn (HINT: type tuple)", "1/4: ___check_type_id(block_mask.mask_mod.__closure__[0].cell_contents, 93981787756512), type=<class 'tuple'> # result = mask_mods[0](b_idx, h_idx, q_idx, k_idx) # ....py:688 in fn (HINT: type tuple)", "1/3: tensor 'block_mask.q_indices' size mismatch at index 2. expected 1, actual 16", "1/2: ___check_obj_id(block_mask.mask_mod.__closure__[1].cell_contents.__closure__[0].cell_contents[0].__code__, 140406789936816), type=<code object fn at 0x7fb300d696b0, file \\"....py\\", line 687> # result = mask_mods[0](b_idx, h_idx, q_idx, k_idx) # .../attention_mask.py:688 in fn", "1/1: ___check_type_id(block_mask.mask_mod.__closure__[0].cell_contents, 93981787756512), type=<class 'tuple'> # result = mask_mods[0](b_idx, h_idx, q_idx, k_idx) # .../attention_mask.py:688 in fn (HINT: type tuple)", "1/0: ___check_type_id(block_mask.mask_mod.__closure__[0].cell_contents, 93981787756512), type=<class 'tuple'> # result = mask_mods[0](b_idx, h_idx, q_idx, k_idx) # ..../attention_mask.py:688 in fn (HINT: type tuple)" ] ``` cc @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @kadeng @amjames @Lucaskabela @jataylo @engineer_01_gh ### Alternatives _No response_ ### Additional context _No response_

title: [dynamo] Include closure cell name in recompilation reasons
labels: high priority, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo, module: guards, dynamo-triage-dec2025
body: Consider the following code: ```python import torch def make_cl(n1, n2): def inner(x): return x + n1 + n2 return inner @torch.compile(backend="eager") def fn(cl, x): return cl(x) fn(make_cl(0, 1), torch.ones(3)) fn(make_cl(0, 2), torch.ones(3)) ``` The recompilation reasons are: ``` $ TORCH_LOGS="recompiles" python playground2.py Recompiling function fn in /data/users/engineer_01/pytorch/playground2.py:9 triggered by the following guard failure(s): - 0/0: cl.__closure__[1].cell_contents == 1 # return x + n1 + n2 # playground2.py:5 in inner - User stack trace: - File "/data/users/engineer_01/pytorch/playground2.py", line 11, in fn - return cl(x) - File "/data/users/engineer_01/pytorch/playground2.py", line 5, in inner - return x + n1 + n2 ``` In this case, it is not clear whether a change to `n1` or `n2` is the cause of the recompilation. We should hint in the recompilation reasons here that `n2` is the variable referred to by `cl.__closure__[1].cell_contents`. cc @engineer_17 @gchanan @kadeng @msaroufim @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @amjames @Lucaskabela @jataylo

title: global dict initialization cause recompilation
labels: feature, triaged, needs design, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug Consider the program below: ```python import torch d = {} @torch.compile(backend="eager", fullgraph=True) def fn(x, key): if key not in d: d[key] = 1 return x.sin() x = torch.randn(2) fn(x, "torch") with torch.compiler.set_stance("fail_on_recompile"): fn(x, "torch") ``` This code uses a common lazy-initialization pattern found in real-world libraries (e.g. [einops](https://github.com/arogozhnikov/einops/blob/43a12ee010a844f3ad57bf404d27e4ee2d151131/einops/_backends.py#L17-L34)). However, under `torch.compile`, it triggers a recompilation on the second execution due to the global dict mutation. For einops this can be "fixed" by initializing the library beforehand, but it would be good if we could have a way to prevent this from happening. ### Error logs _No response_ ### Versions pytorch main cc @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @kadeng @amjames @Lucaskabela @jataylo

title: torch.compile: scalar arithmetic causes unlimited recompiles
labels: oncall: pt2
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug Please run the following code snipped with `TORCH_LOGS=recompiles` What appears to be happening is that torch.compile converts python scalars to tensors and then creates an invalid compile guard against it: `___as_tensor(a).item() == 0.03 # (unknown source ___as_tensor(a).item(), please file a bug)` `compiled_step_fails` therefore recompiles indefinitely. in `compiled_step_works` below there is no scalar arithmetic, but only tensor by scalar. This works as intended: there is only 1 recompile when it detects that `a` is not static, and then it continues with a kernel that can handle that. ``` import torch torch._dynamo.config.cache_size_limit = 8192 @torch.compile(fullgraph=True) def compiled_step_works(p, a, b): p.mul_(p * a * b) @torch.compile(fullgraph=True) def compiled_step_fails(p, a, b): p.mul_(p * (a * b)) a_ = 0.0 b_ = 1.0 device='cuda' p_ = torch.randn(10, device=device) while(True): a_ += 0.01 compiled_step_fails(p_, a_, b_) ``` ### Error logs ``` V1226 11:51:27.633000 3727 venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/guards.py:3508] [0/41] [__recompiles] Recompiling function compiled_step_fails in .../test3.py:10 V1226 11:51:27.633000 3727 venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/guards.py:3508] [0/41] [__recompiles] triggered by the following guard failure(s): V1226 11:51:27.633000 3727 venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/guards.py:3508] [0/41] [__recompiles] - 0/39: ___as_tensor(a).item() == 0.4000000000000002 # (unknown source ___as_tensor(a).item(), please file a bug) V1226 11:51:27.633000 3727 venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/guards.py:3508] [0/41] [__recompiles] - title: Submodule forward hook with kwargs cause recompilation
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo, recompilations, dynamo-triage-dec2025
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug Registering a forward hook on a submodule of a compiled module causes excessive recompilations that hit the recompile limit. When `torch.compile(layer, fullgraph=True)` is applied, each invocation triggers recompilation because the hook id change and cause recompilation. Note that: registering the hook on the compiled module itself does NOT cause recompilation errors, only registering on a submodule within a compiled module does. Repro: https://gist.github.com/fegin/442f58d60b237e6e149ca8f5bbe114c7 ### Versions nightly cc @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @kadeng @amjames @Lucaskabela @jataylo

title: Torch Compile Cache sharing across different GPUs
labels: needs reproduction, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo, compile-cache
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug Hi, I am trying to use `torch.compile` cache to compile the model on one GPU and then reuse the compiled model across different GPUs connected to the same node. For instance, I have compiled a model on GPU0 (rank0) on a 8xH100 machine and then want to make other GPUs on the same node reuse the cache. This is how my code looks like and it doesn’t seem to work. ``` if rank == 0: model_full = Model(apply_regional_compilation=False, hidden_size=hidden_size, num_layers=num_layers).to(device) full_compiled_model = torch.compile(model_full) full_compile_time = compile_only(full_compiled_model, input_tensor, rank, "full") # Warmup (not included in compilation time) warmup(full_compiled_model, input_tensor, rank, num_iters=3) # Signal other ranks that compilation is complete print(f"[Rank {rank}] Full compilation complete, signaling other ranks...") artifacts = torch.compiler.save_cache_artifacts() assert artifacts is not None artifact_bytes, cache_info = artifacts print(f"[Rank {rank}] Cache info: {cache_info}") # Broadcast artifact_bytes from rank 0 to all other ranks artifact_bytes_list = [artifact_bytes] dist.broadcast_object_list(artifact_bytes_list, src=0) else: artifact_bytes_list = [None] dist.broadcast_object_list(artifact_bytes_list, src=0) artifact_bytes = artifact_bytes_list[0] torch.compiler.load_cache_artifacts(artifact_bytes) model_full = Model(apply_regional_compilation=False, hidden_size=hidden_size, num_layers=num_layers).to(device) full_compiled_model = torch.compile(model_full) full_compile_time = compile_only(full_compiled_model, input_tensor, rank, "full (FROM CACHE)") warmup(full_compiled_model, input_tensor, rank, num_iters=3) dist.barrier() ``` I launch the experiment using - `export TORCH_LOGS="+torch._inductor.codecache"; torchrun --standalone --nproc_per_node 2 multi_gpu_compile.py` Looking through the logs generated by `TORCH_LOGS=+torch._inductor.codecache`, I see FX graph has different hashes for each rank and my guess is that’s why every instance of the model on each GPU just goes through the entire compilation process. Is this expected? Is there a way around this - both in a single-node scenario and multi-node scenario? ### Error logs _No response_ ### Versions ``` Collecting environment information... title: torch.compile: Excessive recompilation due to implicit .item() conversion when multiplying scalar and 0-d tensor
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo, recompilations, dynamo-triage-dec2025, PT2-Bug-Bash
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug I encountered an issue where `torch.compile` triggers excessive (or never-ending) recompilations until the limit is reached, eventually raising an error. This occurs specifically when mixing a dynamic Python scalar and a 0-d tensor inside an in-place operation. Consider this line: ```python update.mul_(scaling_factor * lr) ``` **Context:** * `update`: Tensor * `scaling_factor`: A dynamic Python scalar (float) used locally. * `lr`: A dynamic Python scalar (float) that `torch.compile` has tracked as a **0-d tensor** (due to usage elsewhere in the graph). The issue is that when multiplying the Python scalar `scaling_factor` by the 0-d tensor `lr`, `torch.compile` triggers an implicit `.item()` call on `lr` to convert it into a scalar float. Consequently, every time this line is called with a different `lr` value, the guards fail and a full recompilation is triggered. ### Workaround Separating the operations resolves the issue: ```python # This avoids the implicit .item() call and compiles correctly update.mul_(scaling_factor) update.mul_(lr) ``` `torch.compile` should handle this mixed-type multiplication without forcing a conversion to scalar via `.item()`. It should likely treat the operation as if it were separated or automatically promote `scaling_factor` to a 0-d tensor to maintain graph integrity. ### Error logs [0/5] [__recompiles] triggered by the following guard failure(s): V1205 02:52:59.439000 9396 ...\\venv\\Lib\\site-packages\\torch_dynamo\\guards.py:3508] [0/5] [__recompiles] - 0/4: __as_tensor(lr).item() == 8.999999999999999e-05 # (unknown source __as_tensor(lr).item(), please file a bug) V1205 02:52:59.439000 9396 ...\\venv\\Lib\\site-packages\\torch_dynamo\\guards.py:3508] [0/5] [__recompiles] - 0/3: __as_tensor(lr).item() == 6e-05 # (unknown source __as_tensor(lr).item(), please file a bug) V1205 02:52:59.439000 9396 ...\\venv\\Lib\\site-packages\\torch_dynamo\\guards.py:3508] [0/5] [__recompiles] - 0/2: __as_tensor(lr).item() == 3e-05 # (unknown source __as_tensor(lr).item(), please file a bug) V1205 02:52:59.439000 9396 ...\\venv\\Lib\\site-packages\\torch_dynamo\\guards.py:3508] [0/5] [_recompiles] - 0/1: lr == 0.0 V1205 02:52:59.439000 9396 ...\\venv\\Lib\\site-packages\\torch_dynamo\\guards.py:3508] [0/5] [_recompiles] - 0/0: lr == 0.0 ### Versions 2.8.0 cc @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @kadeng @amjames @Lucaskabela @jataylo

title: [compile] Recompilations due to backend mismatch
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug Consider this code where a fresh backend function is created on every instance of `torch.compile` call. This leads to a recompilation issue because `torch.compile` guards on the backend function to check if it can reuse the previously compiled artifact. This is **intentional** because if user want to use two different backends, we want to give two different compiled artifacts. However, in this example this seems accidental on the user side. So, this issue gives you a workaround. ``` import torch from torch._dynamo.backends.common import aot_autograd from functorch.compile import nop def torch_compile_with_custom_backend( module: torch.nn.Module, ): opt_layer = torch.compile( module, backend=aot_autograd(fw_compiler=nop, bw_compiler=nop), fullgraph=True ) return opt_layer class SubMod(torch.nn.Module): def __init__(self): super().__init__() def forward(self, x): return torch.sin(x) class Mod(torch.nn.Module): def __init__(self): super().__init__() self.mod_a = SubMod() self.mod_b = SubMod() self.mod_a = torch_compile_with_custom_backend(self.mod_a) self.mod_b = torch_compile_with_custom_backend(self.mod_b) def forward(self, x): return self.mod_a(x) + self.mod_b(x) mod = Mod() x = torch.randn(4) mod(x) ``` After PR https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/168387 - you will see this type of recompilation message ``` BACKEND_MATCH failure: torch.compile detected different backend callables. If this is unexpected, wrap your backend in functools.partial (or reuse the same cached backend) to avoid creating a new backend function each time. More details: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/168373 ``` **Workaround** - In the above example, the fresh creation of backend function seems accidental. We can fix it by using functools partial. ``` import torch from torch._dynamo.backends.common import aot_autograd from functorch.compile import nop import functools @functools.lru_cache def get_backend(fw_compiler, bw_compiler): return aot_autograd(fw_compiler=nop, bw_compiler=nop) def torch_compile_with_custom_backend( module: torch.nn.Module, ): opt_layer = torch.compile(module, backend=get_backend(nop, nop), fullgraph=True) return opt_layer class SubMod(torch.nn.Module): def __init__(self): super().__init__() def forward(self, x): return torch.sin(x) class Mod(torch.nn.Module): def __init__(self): super().__init__() self.mod_a = SubMod() self.mod_b = SubMod() self.mod_a = torch_compile_with_custom_backend(self.mod_a) self.mod_b = torch_compile_with_custom_backend(self.mod_b) def forward(self, x): return self.mod_a(x) + self.mod_b(x) mod = Mod() x = torch.randn(4) mod(x) ``` ### Error logs _No response_ ### Versions NA cc @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @chenyang78 @kadeng @amjames @Lucaskabela

title: torch._dynamo.exc.FailOnRecompileLimitHit when compiling in a loop
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo, module: compile ux
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug Running the following code, I got `torch._dynamo.exc.FailOnRecompileLimitHit` error. If I remove `fullgraph=True`, the code runs but we still get re-compile warning. But feels like this is legit code with `fullgraph=True` and it should not error out? ``` W1110 17:56:13.321000 77473 torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py:1550] [0/8] torch._dynamo hit config.recompile_limit (8) W1110 17:56:13.321000 77473 torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py:1550] [0/8] function: 'forward' (/data/users/engineer_12/pytorch/test.py:30) W1110 17:56:13.321000 77473 torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py:1550] [0/8] last reason: 0/7: ___check_type_id(self, 224599728) # a = self.net1(x) # test.py:31 in forward W1110 17:56:13.321000 77473 torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py:1550] [0/8] To log all recompilation reasons, use TORCH_LOGS="recompiles". W1110 17:56:13.321000 77473 torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py:1550] [0/8] To diagnose recompilation issues, see https://pytorch.org/docs/main/compile/programming_model.recompilation.html ..... File "/data/users/engineer_12/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py", line 1567, in _compile raise FailOnRecompileLimitHit( torch._dynamo.exc.FailOnRecompileLimitHit: recompile_limit reached with fullgraph=True. Excessive recompilations can degrade performance due to the compilation overhead of each recompilation. To monitor recompilations, enable TORCH_LOGS=recompiles. If recompilations are expected, consider increasing torch._dynamo.config.cache_size_limit to an appropriate value. ``` ```python import torch for i in range(9): class MLPModule(torch.nn.Module): def __init__(self, device): super().__init__() self.net1 = torch.nn.Linear(10, 16, bias=True, device=device) self.relu = torch.nn.ReLU() self.net2 = torch.nn.Linear(16, 10, bias=True, device=device) def forward(self, x): a = self.net1(x) b = self.relu(a) c = self.net2(b) return c device = "cuda" mod = MLPModule(device) compiled = torch.compile(mod, backend="aot_eager", fullgraph=True) result = compiled(torch.randn(10, 10, device=device)) ``` ### Versions nightly cc @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @kadeng @amjames @Lucaskabela @jataylo @chenyang78 @engineer_01_gh @engineer_21_gh

title: recompilation reason due to different backend is empty
labels: high priority, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo, vllm-compile, llm-amenable, module: vllm
body: example: ```python import torch import torch.nn as nn class CustomBackend: """ Custom compile backend class. Each instance can have different state/config. """ def __init__(self, name: str): self.name = name self.call_count = 0 def __call__(self, gm: torch.fx.GraphModule, example_inputs): """ Backend must be callable with (gm, example_inputs) signature. Args: gm: The FX graph module example_inputs: Example inputs to the graph Returns: A compiled callable """ self.call_count += 1 print(f"[{self.name}] Backend called! (call #{self.call_count})") print(f"[{self.name}] Graph: {gm.graph}") print(f"[{self.name}] Number of nodes: {len(list(gm.graph.nodes))}") # You can modify the graph here if needed # For now, just return the graph module which will execute eagerly return gm class MultiplyModule(nn.Module): def __init__(self): super().__init__() def forward(self, x, y): return x * y class B(MultiplyModule): def __init__(self): super().__init__() # Create two instances multiply_module_1 = MultiplyModule() multiply_module_2 = B() # Create compiled versions options = {"guard_filter_fn": lambda x: [False for _ in x]} _compiled_callable1 = torch.compile( multiply_module_1.forward, fullgraph=True, options=options, backend=CustomBackend("hi") ) _compiled_callable2 = torch.compile( multiply_module_2.forward, fullgraph=True, options=options, backend=CustomBackend("bi") ) # Create sample inputs x = torch.tensor([1.0, 2.0, 3.0]) y = torch.tensor([4.0, 5.0, 6.0]) # Run the compiled callables result1 = _compiled_callable1(x, y) result2 = _compiled_callable2(x, y) print("Result from compiled callable 1:", result1) print("Result from compiled callable 2:", result2) ``` recompile reason ``` [ "0/0: " ] ``` For vllm better reason maybe (different backend instance .. etc) is better since in vLLM we expect a different compilation when we pass different instance of the backend but user can get confused by the tlparse https://manifold.edge.x2p.facebook.net/v0/read/tree/logs/engineer_06/71f74e98-0a00-4078-869a-f1803d877dbd/custom/index.html?bucketName=tlparse_reports&apiKey=tlparse_reports-key&withPayload=1&timeoutMsec=10000 example from vllm ``` https://manifold.edge.x2p.facebook.net/v0/read/tree/logs/engineer_06/357dbcc2-8ec0-47ab-944f-7949e7c650cc/custom/index.html?bucketName=tlparse_reports&apiKey=tlparse_reports-key&withPayload=1&timeoutMsec=10000 ``` cc @engineer_17 @gchanan @kadeng @msaroufim @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @chenyang78 @amjames @Lucaskabela @zou3519 @engineer_21_gh

title: combine compiled vectorized function without recompiling already compiled part
labels: triaged, intel, oncall: pt2, module: inductor
body: ### 🚀 The feature, motivation and pitch The nice thing of `torch.compile` is that it fuses the vectorized operations and avoid big intermediate tensors. For example, if I have ``` def func(x): y = f1(x) z = f2(y) return z ``` After `torch.compile` it becomes something like ``` for(int i=0;i<len(x);i++) { tmp_scalar = f1(x[i]) z[i] = f2(tmp_scalar) } ``` However if `f1` and `f2` are big functions, it expands everything inside. Is there a way to prevent the expansion of `f1` and `f2`, while still keeping the fusing behavior, for the purpose of reducing compilation time? In C++, `f1` and `f2` should be compiled into a non-inlined scalar function, and I would just like to do another compilation to combine `f1` and `f2` and then loop over `i`. If I understand correctly, graph break does not try to fuse the separated parts, although it can avoid recompilation. ### Alternatives _No response_ ### Additional context _No response_ cc @jgong5 @mingfeima @XiaobingSuper @sanchitintel @ashokei @jingxu10 @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @Guobing-Chen @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @chenyang78 @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben

title: [Inductor][AO] Flex Attention with AO hit the recompile limit on `v2.9.0`
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: flex attention
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug On `v2.9.0` tag, models using flex attention with ao hit the `recompile_limit`. It prevents flex attention from being properly fused. As a result, the prefill e2e performance drops to about 35% of the main branch. The log when this issue occurs is as follows: ``` W1021 16:19:11.680000 2300716 /pytorch/xingyuan/projects/20251020-pytorch28-validate/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py:1364] [13/8] torch._dynamo hit config.recompile_limit (8) W1021 16:19:11.680000 2300716 /pytorch/xingyuan/projects/20251020-pytorch28-validate/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py:1364] [13/8] function: 'flex_attention' (/pytorch/xingyuan/projects/20251020-pytorch28-validate/pytorch/torch/nn/attention/flex_attention.py:1440) W1021 16:19:11.680000 2300716 /pytorch/xingyuan/projects/20251020-pytorch28-validate/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py:1364] [13/8] last reason: 13/7: ___check_obj_id(key, 123962271811680) # if query.dtype != key.dtype or query.dtype != value.dtype: # nn/attention/_utils.py:44 in _validate_sdpa_input W1021 16:19:11.680000 2300716 /pytorch/xingyuan/projects/20251020-pytorch28-validate/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py:1364] [13/8] To log all recompilation reasons, use TORCH_LOGS="recompiles". W1021 16:19:11.680000 2300716 /pytorch/xingyuan/projects/20251020-pytorch28-validate/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py:1364] [13/8] To diagnose recompilation issues, see https://pytorch.org/docs/main/torch.compiler_troubleshooting.html /pytorch/xingyuan/projects/20251020-pytorch28-validate/pytorch/torch/nn/attention/flex_attention.py:1678: UserWarning: flex_attention called without torch.compile() - this will use an unfused implementation that materializes the full scores matrix instead of generating a fused kernel. ``` After investigation, we found that this bug has been fixed in commit 75de5b6. Additionally, we tried to cherry-pick 75de5b6 on `v2.9.0`, and this issue was successfully resolved. ### Versions ``` Collecting environment information... title: [precompile] AOT Precompile serialization failed when running mutliple times
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, oncall: export, module: aotinductor
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug Create a simple python script `precompile_repro.py` ```python import torch class M(torch.nn.Module): def __init__(self): super().__init__() self.linear1 = torch.nn.Linear(2, 4) self.relu = torch.nn.ReLU() self.linear2 = torch.nn.Linear(4, 8) def forward(self, x): return self.linear2(self.relu(self.linear1(x))) device = "cuda" m = M().to(device) sample_inputs = (torch.randn(2, 2, device=device),) eager_out = m(*sample_inputs) with torch._dynamo.config.patch("enable_aot_compile", True): compiled_fn_path = "./m.pt" compiled_fn = torch.compile( m, fullgraph=True ).forward.aot_compile((sample_inputs, {})) compiled_fn.save_compiled_function(compiled_fn_path) torch._dynamo.reset() with torch.compiler.set_stance("fail_on_recompile"): with open(compiled_fn_path, "rb") as f: loaded_fn = torch.compiler.load_compiled_function(f) assert loaded_fn is not None compiled_out = loaded_fn(m, *sample_inputs). assert torch.allclose(eager_out, compiled_out) ``` Then in terminal run `python precompile_repro.py`. It works the first time. Then if running this script again, we will get ``` File "/data/users/yimingzhou/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/aot_compile.py", line 118, in save_compiled_function f.write(type(self).serialize(self)) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/data/users/yimingzhou/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/aot_compile.py", line 130, in serialize type(compiled_fn).serialize_compile_artifacts(compiled_fn), ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/data/users/yimingzhou/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/aot_compile.py", line 181, in serialize_compile_artifacts result = pickle.dumps(fn.compiled_fn.serialize()) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ AttributeError: Can't get local object 'align_inputs_from_check_idxs.<locals>.run' ``` If the device is `cpu`, then running the script the second will get the following error ``` File "/data/users/yimingzhou/pytorch/precompile-repro.py", line 25, in <module> compiled_fn.save_compiled_function(compiled_fn_path) File "/data/users/yimingzhou/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/aot_compile.py", line 118, in save_compiled_function f.write(type(self).serialize(self)) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/data/users/yimingzhou/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/aot_compile.py", line 130, in serialize type(compiled_fn).serialize_compile_artifacts(compiled_fn), ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/data/users/yimingzhou/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/aot_compile.py", line 181, in serialize_compile_artifacts result = pickle.dumps(fn.compiled_fn.serialize()) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ AttributeError: Can't get local object 'WeakValueDictionary.__init__.<locals>.remove' ``` The error is gone when adding `fresh_cache()` context manager from `torch._inductor.utils` ```python with fresh_cache(), torch._dynamo.config.patch("enable_aot_compile", True): ....aot_compile... ``` Then we can the script multiple times and it always works on both cpu and cuda. ### Versions main cc @chauhang @penguinwu @avikchaudhuri @gmagogsfm @engineer_08 @tugsbayasgalan @engineer_05 @suo @ydwu4 @desertfire @chenyang78 @yushangdi @benjaminglass1 @jamesjwu

labels: needs reproduction, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug Hello, In some private workload I am running (unfortunately I don't have a minimal repro - I can try to get one if needed), the recompilation cause: ``` V1009 11:33:51.404000 3024 site-packages/torch/_dynamo/guards.py:3006] [0/5] [__recompiles] Recompiling function inner in /root/miniforge3/lib/python3.12/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/external_utils.py:68 V1009 11:33:51.404000 3024 site-packages/torch/_dynamo/guards.py:3006] [0/5] [__recompiles] triggered by the following guard failure(s): V1009 11:33:51.404000 3024 site-packages/torch/_dynamo/guards.py:3006] [0/5] [__recompiles] - 0/1: expected type of 'args[1]' to be a tensor type, ' but found <class 'torch.Tensor'> ``` gets printed. title: Unexpected recompilation under `TorchDispatchMode` with `is_infra_mode` set to `True`
labels: triaged, module: dispatch, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug The test case to reproduce the problem is shown below. If I don't put the forward call under the dispatch mode, there would be no errors reported. ```python import torch from torch.utils._python_dispatch import TorchDispatchMode class MyTorchDispatchMode(TorchDispatchMode): @classmethod def is_infra_mode(cls): return True def __torch_dispatch__(self, func, types, args=(), kwargs=None): kwargs = {} if kwargs is None else kwargs out = func(*args, **kwargs) return out def fn(x): return x + 2 torch._dynamo.config.recompile_limit = 1 torch.compiler.reset() fn_compiled = torch.compile(fn, backend="eager", dynamic=True, fullgraph=True) for bs in [2, 4, 8, 16]: x = torch.randn(bs, 4) with ( MyTorchDispatchMode(), # no errors if I remove this context torch.no_grad(), ): out = fn_compiled(x) ``` ### Error logs return fn(*args, **kwargs) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/home/jgong5/miniforge3/envs/py312/lib/python3.12/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py", line 1495, in __call__ return self._torchdynamo_orig_callable( ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/home/jgong5/miniforge3/envs/py312/lib/python3.12/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py", line 629, in __call__ return _compile( ^^^^^^^^^ File "/home/jgong5/miniforge3/envs/py312/lib/python3.12/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py", line 1033, in _compile raise FailOnRecompileLimitHit( torch._dynamo.exc.FailOnRecompileLimitHit: recompile_limit reached with one_graph=True. Excessive recompilations can degrade performance due to the compilation overhead of each recompilation. To monitor recompilations, enable TORCH_LOGS=recompiles. If recompilations are expected, consider increasing torch._dynamo.config.cache_size_limit to an appropriate value. ### Versions Collecting environment information... title: [dynamo] recompilation failure error message still points to old troubleshooting doc
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo, easy, module: compile ux
body: e.g. ``` W0922 00:45:54.324000 2065324 /data/users/engineer_01/pytorch2/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py:1364] [0/8] torch._dynamo hit config.recompile_limit (8) W0922 00:45:54.324000 2065324 /data/users/engineer_01/pytorch2/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py:1364] [0/8] function: 'inner' (/data/users/engineer_01/pytorch2/torch/_dynamo/external_utils.py:66) W0922 00:45:54.324000 2065324 /data/users/engineer_01/pytorch2/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py:1364] [0/8] last reason: 0/1: ___check_obj_id(fn.__code__, 140655353128048) W0922 00:45:54.324000 2065324 /data/users/engineer_01/pytorch2/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py:1364] [0/8] To log all recompilation reasons, use TORCH_LOGS="recompiles". W0922 00:45:54.324000 2065324 /data/users/engineer_01/pytorch2/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py:1364] [0/8] To diagnose recompilation issues, see https://pytorch.org/docs/main/torch.compiler_troubleshooting.htm ``` We should be pointing to the new programming model docs. (Also check where else in the repo this link is still present). cc @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @chenyang78 @kadeng @amjames @Lucaskabela

title: [dynamo] Show recompilation reason when set_stance("fail_on_recompile") fails
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo, module: compile ux
body: Today, even with `TORCH_LOGS=recompiles`, `set_stance("fail_on_recompile")` failures do not provide the reason why the recompilation occurred, e.g. ``` File "/data/users/engineer_01/pytorch2/torch/_dynamo/eval_frame.py", line 251, in fail_callback raise RuntimeError(message) RuntimeError: Detected recompile when torch.compile stance is 'fail_on_recompile'. filename: '/data/users/engineer_01/pytorch2/torch/_dynamo/external_utils.py', function name: 'inner', line number: 66 ``` We should show the recompilation reason normally logged to `TORCH_LOGS=recompiles`, similar to when the recompilation limit is hit. cc @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @chenyang78 @kadeng @amjames @Lucaskabela

title: Potential bug in _inductor/runtime/cache_dir_utils.py
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, compile-cache
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug When TRITON_CACHE_DIR env is set, the device id is not appended to the cache directory (https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/33e6c5a93d4e249d3a05cbe434877b8a77922a90/torch/_inductor/runtime/cache_dir_utils.py#L31) This might result in a failure on this line (https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/33e6c5a93d4e249d3a05cbe434877b8a77922a90/torch/_inductor/triton_bundler.py#L394) with multiple ranks trying to replace to the same trajectory. I can send a fix, but I want to understand first if this is my misunderstanding of the docs, and we expect TRITON_CACHE_DIR per rank. Or (which will be more consistent with the code path with the unset TRITON_CACHE_DIR, we will append the str(device) to avoid the crash. I do not have a small reproducible example rn, but the crash I get is on the os.replace() call with this: ```bash torch._dynamo.exc.BackendCompilerFailed: backend='inductor' raised: [rank3]: OSError: [Errno 39] Directory not empty: ``` ### Versions ```bash torch==2.8.0a0+5228986c39.nv25.6 ``` cc @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @chenyang78 @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben @oulgen @jamesjwu @masnesral

title: torch.compile: Segfault or "Unhandled FakeTensor Device Propagation" when compiling a Module on CPU, moving it to CUDA, recompiling, then calling with a CPU input
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: fakeTensor, module: dynamo, module: guards, module: pt2-dispatcher
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug ### Minimal Repro Script ```python import os import torch import torch.nn as nn import torch.nn.functional as F # optional: pin to a single GPU for clarity os.environ.setdefault("CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES", "0") class MyModel(nn.Module): def __init__(self): super().__init__() self.linear = nn.Linear(10, 4) def forward(self, x): return self.linear(x) def my_model_function(): return MyModel() def GetInput(): # CPU input return torch.randn(1, 10, requires_grad=True) def repro(case="A"): model = my_model_function() input_tensor = GetInput() # CPU tensor _ = model(input_tensor) # eager CPU run OK # Compile on CPU compiled_cpu = torch.compile(model) if case == "B": # Calling the CPU-compiled function once seems to change the behavior later _ = compiled_cpu(input_tensor) # OK on CPU # Move the SAME module instance to CUDA, and compile again model.to("cuda") print("before second compile") compiled_cuda = torch.compile(model) print("after second compile") # Now call CUDA-compiled function with a CPU input on purpose # Expectation: a clear "device mismatch" style error # Actual: either FakeTensor device propagation error (case A), # or a segmentation fault (case B). out = compiled_cuda(input_tensor) if __name__ == "__main__": # Case A: Do NOT call the first compiled function before moving to CUDA # -> raises: TorchRuntimeError: Unhandled FakeTensor Device Propagation ... try: repro("A") except Exception as e: print("Case A raised:", repr(e)) # Case B: Call the CPU-compiled function once before moving to CUDA # -> causes a Segmentation fault (core dumped) on my setup # Comment this in/out to observe the difference: repro("B") ``` ### Observed Behavior + Case A (do not call the first compiled fn before moving to CUDA): Fails with: ``` TorchRuntimeError: Dynamo failed to run FX node ... Unhandled FakeTensor Device Propagation for aten.mm.default, found two different devices cpu, cuda:0 ``` (Raised from the `addmm`/`mm` decomposition while merging device across FakeTensors.) + Case B (call the CPU-compiled fn once, then move module to CUDA, recompile, and call with CPU input): Segmentation fault (core dumped) ``` before second compile after second compile Case A raised: TorchRuntimeError('Dynamo failed to run FX node with fake tensors: call_function <built-in function linear>(*(FakeTensor(..., size=(1, 10), requires_grad=True), Parameter(FakeTensor(..., device=\\'cuda:0\\', size=(4, 10), requires_grad=True)), Parameter(FakeTensor(..., device=\\'cuda:0\\', size=(4,), requires_grad=True))), **{}): got RuntimeError(\\'Unhandled FakeTensor Device Propagation for aten.mm.default, found two different devices cpu, cuda:0\\')\\n\\nfrom user code:\\n File "xxx/for_test.py", line 15, in forward\\n return self.linear(x)\\n File "xxx/pytorch-main/torch/nn/modules/linear.py", line 134, in forward\\n return F.linear(input, self.weight, self.bias)\\n\\nSet TORCHDYNAMO_VERBOSE=1 for the internal stack trace (please do this especially if you\\'re reporting a bug to PyTorch). For even more developer context, set TORCH_LOGS="+dynamo"\\n') before second compile after second compile Segmentation fault (core dumped) ``` ### Versions ``` PyTorch version: 2.8.0a0+gitba56102 & 2.9.0a0+git31d5c67 Is debug build: True CUDA used to build PyTorch: 12.6 ROCM used to build PyTorch: N/A OS: Ubuntu 22.04.4 LTS (x86_64) GCC version: (Ubuntu 12.3.0-1ubuntu1~22.04) 12.3.0 Clang version: Could not collect CMake version: version 3.22.1 Libc version: glibc-2.35 Python version: 3.10.16 (main, Dec 11 2024, 16:24:50) [GCC 11.2.0] (64-bit runtime) Python platform: Linux-6.8.0-59-generic-x86_64-with-glibc2.35 Is CUDA available: True ``` cc @chauhang @penguinwu @engineer_02 @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @chenyang78 @kadeng @amjames @Lucaskabela @zou3519 @bdhirsh

title: Compilation time debugging: construction sympy.Min/Max can be very slow
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamic shapes, module: compile-time
body: I was debugging the compilation time increase from LOAF and found that it's very slow to construct sympy.Min/Max. We would construct such objects when we call sympy_subs on an expression containing Min/Max. It's quite common especially for timm models which contains pooling ops. The reason for the slowness of sympy.Min/Max is sympy would try to optimize the expression so if it knows for certain that 'a < b' than it will decide Min(a, b) as 'a'. But often times, when `a` or `b` contains free symbols, such optimization takes time but does not having any effect. Just get a feeling about how slow it is. When compiling the bwd graph of timm inception_v3 , 45% of the time is spend on sympy_subs ( scuba link: https://fburl.com/scuba/pyperf_experimental/on_demand/8yj4e610 ). The number may be exaggerated due to distortion from profiling. But overall it shows we spend a lot of time on sympy_subst on this model. And most of the time, sympy_subst is optimizing the Min/Max nodes. I created a patch and added it into the top and compile_fx.py to setup the experiments ``` if os.getenv("PATCH_SYMPY_MIN_MAX") == "1": def _patch_sympy_min_max(): from sympy.functions.elementary.miscellaneous import MinMaxBase original = MinMaxBase._find_localzeros.__func__ def new_find_localzeros(cls, values, **options): if not isinstance(values, (tuple, list)): values = tuple(values) if len(values) == 2 and any(bool(x.free_symbols) for x in values): # skip the simplification below # This may miss some simplication opportunities like # Min(x + 1, x + 2) # but it may worth it by reducing compilation time return values return original(cls, values, **options) MinMaxBase._find_localzeros = classmethod(new_find_localzeros) _patch_sympy_min_max() ``` I run the inception_v3 model 4 times and collect the compilation time (raw logs: https://gist.github.com/engineer_18_gh/7b8a5da9a90c41fba52996c2e4c294fb ) - no-patch, no LOAF: 98.29s - no-patch, with LOAF: 102.06s - with-patch, no LOAF: 83.22s - with-patch, with LOAF: 89.6s So the patch can reduce compilation time a lot for this model. (1.1x to 1.2x) I'm wondering how people think about this. I'd also like to hear if people know better ways than monkey patch for such optimization. cc @chauhang @penguinwu @engineer_17 @engineer_15_gh @oulgen @jamesjwu @engineer_03 @engineer_21_gh @laithsakka @masnesral @coconutruben @engineer_14 , @engineer_02 cc @chauhang @penguinwu @oulgen @jamesjwu @engineer_03 @engineer_21_gh @laithsakka @masnesral @coconutruben

title: RTX 5060 Ti (sm_120) GPU crashes: missing precompiled CUDA kernels in PyTorch
labels: oncall: pt2
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug Hi PyTorch Team, I am trying to run Stable Diffusion on my NVIDIA RTX 5060 Ti (sm_120) GPU. PyTorch detects the GPU correctly, but the current build does not include precompiled CUDA kernels for sm_120. As a result, any GPU computation crashes. Here are the details: - GPU: NVIDIA RTX 5060 Ti (sm_120) - OS: [Your OS and version, e.g., Windows 11 / Ubuntu 22.04] - PyTorch version: [e.g., 2.2.0] - CUDA version: [e.g., 12.1] - Steps to reproduce: Run any GPU PyTorch script, e.g., Stable Diffusion, with `device='cuda'`. - Error / Logs: [Attach any error messages or stack traces] This GPU is a newer architecture and requires precompiled CUDA kernels in PyTorch to run computations correctly. Could you please add support for sm_120 in an upcoming release or advise on a workaround? Thank you for your help! ### Error logs RuntimeError: CUDA error: no kernel image is available for execution on the device CUDA kernel errors might be asynchronously reported at some other API call, so the stacktrace below might be incorrect. For debugging consider passing CUDA_LAUNCH_BLOCKING=1. Compile with TORCH_USE_CUDA_DSA to enable device-side assertions. (venv) C:\\Users\\sales\\OneDrive\\Desktop\\AI\\stable-diffusion-webui>python -c "import torch; print(torch.__version__, torch.cuda.is_available(), torch.cuda.get_device_name(0))" C:\\Users\\sales\\OneDrive\\Desktop\\AI\\stable-diffusion-webui\\venv\\lib\\site-packages\\torch\\cuda\\__init__.py:209: UserWarning: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5060 Ti with CUDA capability sm_120 is not compatible with the current PyTorch installation. The current PyTorch install supports CUDA capabilities sm_50 sm_60 sm_61 sm_70 sm_75 sm_80 sm_86 sm_90. If you want to use the NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5060 Ti GPU with PyTorch, please check the instructions at https://pytorch.org/get-started/locally/ warnings.warn( 2.3.0+cu121 True NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5060 Ti ### Versions 2.3.0+cu121 True NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5060 Ti cc @chauhang @penguinwu

title: inductor's process pool seems to time out while cleaning up after a cold start
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: inductor
body: When I run this basic script below, it runs for 5 minutes before timing out. This is on an H100 devgpu. From some local debugging, I see that the number of processes we spawn is determined by `min(32, len(os.sched_getaffinity(0)))`([code](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/main/torch/_inductor/config.py#L859)), which for me returns 384 so compile spawns 32 threads for me. output: ``` (pytorch) [hirsheybar@devgpu034.ldc1 ~/local/new2/pytorch (view_replay_reland)]$ time python tmp5.py Exception ignored in atexit callback: <function shutdown_compile_workers at 0x7f888065ca40> Traceback (most recent call last): File "/data/users/hirsheybar/new2/pytorch/torch/_inductor/async_compile.py", line 146, in shutdown_compile_workers pool.shutdown() File "/data/users/hirsheybar/new2/pytorch/torch/_inductor/compile_worker/subproc_pool.py", line 269, in shutdown self.process.wait(300) File "/home/hirsheybar/.conda/envs/pytorch/lib/python3.12/subprocess.py", line 1264, in wait return self._wait(timeout=timeout) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/home/hirsheybar/.conda/envs/pytorch/lib/python3.12/subprocess.py", line 2038, in _wait raise TimeoutExpired(self.args, timeout) subprocess.TimeoutExpired: Command '['/home/hirsheybar/.conda/envs/pytorch/bin/python3', '/data/users/hirsheybar/new2/pytorch/torch/_inductor/compile_worker/__main__.py', '--pickler=torch._inductor.compile_worker.subproc_pool.SubprocPickler', '--kind=fork', '--workers=32', '--parent=293703', '--read-fd=80', '--write-fd=83', '--torch-key=e6p/L7C0zXOqh9YwNiGl8VY/FA9gq5k4be43K+G9zYI=']' timed out after 300 seconds real 5m7.924s user 0m13.466s sys 0m1.605s ``` script: ``` import torch @torch.compile def f(x): return x.sin().sin() x = torch.randn(2, device='cuda') out = f(x) ``` cc @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @chenyang78 @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben

title: [UED][Falcon-7B x vLLM] excessive shape recompilations (static specialization)
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamic shapes, module: dynamo, empathy-day, vllm-compile, module: vllm
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug Following #161111, #161113, #161115, we've dynamo-disabled 2 causal_conv1d triton kernels, and the selective_scan_fwd custom op. `fullgraph=False` compilation is fine, and accuracy looks good. Just reporting that compilation doesn't turn dynamic, and several static compilations happen; might be interesting to see why they don't compile dynamically. title: PT2 Cache <> CustomOp issue
labels: triaged, module: custom-operators, oncall: pt2, module: pt2-dispatcher, compile-cache
body: PT2 cache does not capture changes in CustomOp. ### Repro First run this code with `python example.py`, and observe "CUDA error: an illegal memory access was encountered". This error is expected since the fake implementation gives wrong dtype of the first output. ```python import torch @torch.library.custom_op("ads_mkl::goo", mutates_args=()) def goo( x: torch.Tensor, weight: torch.Tensor, eps: float ) -> tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]: # x: bfloat16, w: float32 # return out: bfloat16, rstd: float32 return x.to(torch.bfloat16)+1, torch.randn(x.shape[0], dtype=torch.float32) @goo.register_fake def _(x, weight, eps=1e-5): rms = torch.sqrt(eps + torch.sum(x * x, dim=1) / x.shape[1]) rstd = 1 / rms return ((x * rstd[:, None] * weight[None, :])), rstd # Wrong # return ((x * rstd[:, None] * weight[None, :])).to(torch.bfloat16), rstd # Correct @torch.compile def run(x, w, eps): out = goo( x, w, eps, )[0] return out + x x = torch.randn((1024 * 128, 512), dtype=torch.bfloat16, device="cuda") w = torch.randn(512, dtype=torch.float32, device="cuda") run(x, w, 1e-5) ``` Then, replace `return ((x * rstd[:, None] * weight[None, :])), rstd # Wrong` with `return ((x * rstd[:, None] * weight[None, :])).to(torch.bfloat16), rstd # Correct`, and run `python example.py` again. We will see the same `CUDA error: an illegal memory access was encountered`. This should NOT happen since the fake implementation is correct now. Without any code changes, if we run `TORCHINDUCTOR_FORCE_DISABLE_CACHES=1 python3 example.py`, there is no error. cc @chauhang @penguinwu @zou3519 @bdhirsh @oulgen @jamesjwu @masnesral

title: torch.compile nightly: Warning spam from deprecated TF32 API
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, compile-cache
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug We added a new deprecation warning in: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/158209. And it's being triggered by code cache internals on every new process. ```python /home/engineer_23/core/a/pytorch/torch/backends/cuda/__init__.py:132: UserWarning: Please use the new API settings to control TF32 behavior, such as torch.backends.cudnn.conv.fp32_precision = 'tf32' or torch.backends.cuda.matmul.fp32_precision = 'ieee'. Old settings, e.g, torch.backends.cuda.matmul.allow_tf32 = True, torch.backends.cudnn.allow_tf32 = True, allowTF32CuDNN() and allowTF32CuBLAS() will be deprecated after Pytorch 2.9. Please see https://pytorch.org/docs/main/notes/cuda.html#tensorfloat-32-tf32-on-ampere-and-later-devices ``` from: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/846ada49738cc4d381da53cf0966b9af3d6bcdbb/torch/_inductor/codecache.py#L819-L821 ### Versions main cc @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @chenyang78 @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben @oulgen @jamesjwu @masnesral

title: Erroneous Recompile Warnings with Multiple Models
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo, module: compile ux
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug The following unexpectedly produces a recompile error. ``` import torch print(torch.__version__) torch._logging.set_logs(recompiles=True) torch._dynamo.reset() torch._dynamo.config.recompile_limit = 1 torch._dynamo.config.fail_on_recompile_limit_hit = True class MyMod(torch.nn.Module): def __init__(self, a, b): super().__init__() self.linear = torch.nn.Linear(a, b) def forward(self, x): return self.linear(x) M1 = MyMod(16, 32) M2 = MyMod(8, 64) M1 = torch.compile(M1) M2 = torch.compile(M2) x1 = torch.randn(512, 16) x2 = torch.randn(10, 8) M1(x1) M2(x2) ``` ### Error logs 2.8.0.dev20250609+cu118 V0722 21:56:30.043000 601 torch/_dynamo/guards.py:3489] [0/1] [__recompiles] Recompiling function forward in /tmp/ipython-input-4-2455819522.py:13 V0722 21:56:30.043000 601 torch/_dynamo/guards.py:3489] [0/1] [__recompiles] triggered by the following guard failure(s): V0722 21:56:30.043000 601 torch/_dynamo/guards.py:3489] [0/1] [__recompiles] - 0/0: tensor 'x' size mismatch at index 0. expected 512, actual 10 W0722 21:56:30.044000 601 torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py:1002] [0/1] torch._dynamo hit config.recompile_limit (1) W0722 21:56:30.044000 601 torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py:1002] [0/1] function: 'forward' (/tmp/ipython-input-4-2455819522.py:13) W0722 21:56:30.044000 601 torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py:1002] [0/1] last reason: 0/0: tensor 'x' size mismatch at index 0. expected 512, actual 10 W0722 21:56:30.044000 601 torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py:1002] [0/1] To log all recompilation reasons, use TORCH_LOGS="recompiles". W0722 21:56:30.044000 601 torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py:1002] [0/1] To diagnose recompilation issues, see https://pytorch.org/docs/main/torch.compiler_troubleshooting.html. --------------------------------------------------------------------------- FailOnRecompileLimitHit Traceback (most recent call last) [/tmp/ipython-input-4-2455819522.py](https://localhost:8080/#) in <cell line: 0>() 25 26 M1(x1) ---> 27 M2(x2) 9 frames [/usr/local/lib/python3.11/dist-packages/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py](https://localhost:8080/#) in _compile(code, globals, locals, builtins, closure, compiler_fn, one_graph, export, export_constraints, hooks, cache_entry, cache_size, frame, frame_state, compile_id, skip) 1013 ) 1014 if config.fail_on_recompile_limit_hit: -> 1015 raise FailOnRecompileLimitHit( 1016 f"{limit_type} reached, because fail_on_recompile_limit_hit = True this is a HARD failure" 1017 ) FailOnRecompileLimitHit: recompile_limit reached, because fail_on_recompile_limit_hit = True this is a HARD failure ### Versions Collecting environment information... title: Consider changing AOTAutograd cache to hit on graphs with different input and node names
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: compile-time, compile-cache, vllm-compile, module: vllm
body: We ran into this in vLLM, where vLLM: 1) creates one big graph with a lot of transformer layers 2) splits the big graph into multiple graphs, each of which are the same 3) compiles each graph separately (sending it through AOTAutograd). All of these graphs cache miss due to having different input names. The workaround I am currently applying is to normalize the inputs of the graphs before sending them through AOTAutograd. It's not clear to me if the right long-term solution is: 1) AOTAutograd cache becomes agnostic to names 2) user is supposed to do some sort of hierarchical compilation 3) user is supposed to know these quirks with AOTAutograd cache and program with those in mind cc @chauhang @penguinwu @oulgen @jamesjwu @engineer_03 @engineer_21_gh @laithsakka @masnesral @coconutruben

title: [dynamo] using disable inside of compile always recompiles
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug This below snippet always recompiles ```python import torch @torch.compile(backend="eager") def main(x): @torch._dynamo.disable def fn(x): return x + 10 return fn(x) x = torch.randn(10, 10) torch._dynamo.config.error_on_recompile = True for _ in range(2): main(x) ``` ```python /home/engineer_23/core/a/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/pgo.py:525: UserWarning: dynamo_pgo force disabled by torch._inductor.config.force_disable_caches warn_once( Traceback (most recent call last): File "/home/engineer_23/core/a/pytorch/standalone.py", line 17, in <module> main(x) File "/home/engineer_23/core/a/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/eval_frame.py", line 749, in compile_wrapper return fn(*args, **kwargs) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/home/engineer_23/core/a/pytorch/standalone.py", line 7, in main @torch._dynamo.disable File "/home/engineer_23/core/a/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py", line 1562, in __call__ return self._torchdynamo_orig_callable( ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/home/engineer_23/core/a/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py", line 1320, in __call__ result = self._inner_convert( ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/home/engineer_23/core/a/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py", line 648, in __call__ return _compile( ^^^^^^^^^ File "/home/engineer_23/core/a/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py", line 1029, in _compile reasons = get_and_maybe_log_recompilation_reasons(cache_entry, frame) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/home/engineer_23/core/a/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/guards.py", line 3499, in get_and_maybe_log_recompilation_reasons raise exc.RecompileError(message) torch._dynamo.exc.RecompileError: Recompiling function torch_dynamo_resume_in_main_at_7 in /home/engineer_23/core/a/pytorch/standalone.py:7 triggered by the following guard failure(s): - 1/0: Cache line invalidated because L['___stack0'] got deallocated ``` There is a workaround to define the disable annotated function outside, but this can be a bit annoying to do for functions with closures ### Versions main cc @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @chenyang78 @kadeng @amjames

title: [user empathy] compile for `transformers` model
labels: triaged, module: cuda graphs, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo, module: compile-time
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug I was looking at the current integration of compile for `transformers` repo. One of my main goal was to understand if we can reduce the compile time by moving `torch.compile` to a repeated block, instead of the full model. In diffusion models, this strategy leads to 8x-10x reduction in compile time w/o sacrificing perf benefits compared to full model compilation (data in this [PR](https://github.com/huggingface/diffusers/pull/11705)). So, naturally I was curious about LLMs, because if true, it can significantly improve ux/adoption. But I was hit with multiple issues. This issue tries to create a small test case to bring those issues forward. ``` import torch class Cache: def __init__(self, num_layers): self.key_cache = [] self.value_cache = [] for i in range(num_layers): k = torch.zeros(4, 4, device="cuda") v = torch.zeros(4, 4, device="cuda") torch._dynamo.mark_static_address(k) torch._dynamo.mark_static_address(v) self.key_cache.append(k) self.value_cache.append(v) def get(self, idx): return self.key_value[idx], self.value_cache[idx] def update(self, new_k, new_v, idx): self.key_cache[idx].copy_(new_k) self.value_cache[idx].copy_(new_v) return self.key_cache[idx], self.value_cache[idx] class Transformer(torch.nn.Module): def __init__(self, layer_idx): super().__init__() self.layer_idx = layer_idx def forward(self, x, cache): k = torch.sin(x) v = torch.cos(x) k, v = cache.update(k, v, self.layer_idx) return k + v class Model(torch.nn.Module): def __init__(self): super().__init__() self.num_layers = 16 self.layers = [Transformer(i) for i in range(self.num_layers)] self.cache = Cache(self.num_layers) def forward(self, x): for i in range(self.num_layers): x = self.layers[i](x, self.cache) return x mod= Model().cuda() x = torch.randn(4, 4, device="cuda") mod(x) def full_model_compile(): # This works # https://manifold.edge.x2p.facebook.net/v0/read/tree/logs/.tmpsKSTQ8/index.html?bucketName=tlparse_reports&apiKey=tlparse_reports-key&withPayload=1&timeoutMsec=10000 opt_mod = torch.compile(mod, mode="reduce-overhead", fullgraph=True) opt_mod(x) # full_model_compile() def transformer_compile(): for submod in mod.layers: submod.compile(fullgraph=True) # submod.compile(fullgraph=True, mode="reduce-overhead") mod(x) torch.compiler.reset() transformer_compile() ``` In the above repro, full model works. This is also the current integration point, but the compile time issue has been raised multiple times. The function `transformer_compile` compiles just the transformer layers. There are the problems - [ ] Too many recompilations on `self.layer_idx` - `last reason: 0/7: self.layer_idx == 7`. The interesting thing in this case is the graph is same for all layer_ids, but the pre-graph bytecode to extract the tensor is different. This might be very hard to fix completely in dynamo. - [ ] cudagraph issue (cc @mcarilli @engineer_17 @engineer_02 @penguinwu @BoyuanFeng @chauhang @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @chenyang78 @kadeng @amjames @oulgen @jamesjwu @engineer_03 @laithsakka @masnesral). This issues exists in `diffusers` models. ``` RuntimeError: Error: accessing tensor output of CUDAGraphs that has been overwritten by a subsequent run. Stack trace: File "/home/engineer_21/local/pytorch/examples/hf_gen_example.py", line 34, in forward return k + v. To prevent overwriting, clone the tensor outside of torch.compile() or call torch.compiler.cudagraph_mark_step_begin() before each model invocation. ``` - [ ] Recompilations because of `mark_static_address` - I attempted to remove the `self.layer_idx` guard by refactoring `Transformer` method to `read` the `past_k` and `past_v` values from the cache, and then directly do `copy_` on them later on (instead of using `self.layer_idx`). Something like this ``` class TransformerFailedAttempt(torch.nn.Module): def __init__(self, layer_idx): super().__init__() self.layer_idx = layer_idx @torch.compile def _forward(self, x, past_k, past_v): k = torch.sin(x) v = torch.cos(x) k, v = cache.update_with_pastkv(k, v, past_k, past_v) return k + v def forward(self, x, cache): past_k, past_v = cache.get(self.layer_idx) return self._forward(x, past_k, past_v) ``` But this too failed because `mark_static_address` guards on the ID of the tensors, resulting in id guard failures on `past_k` and `past_v`. This is discussed at https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/156377 Note - I tried 3 models - Gemma3, Gemma2 and Qwen3 (ok effectively 2). All suffer from same exact issues, so I think the many models will suffer the same fate. ### Error logs cc @chauhang @penguinwu @zou3519 @BoyuanFeng @engineer_02 @StrongerXi @engineer_17 cc @gante for `transformers` ### Versions nightly

title: [compile][transformers] Recompilation with mark_static_address with cudagraphs
labels: triaged, module: cuda graphs, oncall: pt2
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug The recompilation happens because we ID_MATCH on the tensors with mark_static_address. I think this is done because cudagraphs can then don't have to copy them into a separate location before running the cudagraph recording. And Dynamo wants to reflect that mapping by guarding on ID. This makes sense. But I was wondering - if we can avoid this recompilation? I think we already have logic that allows us multiple cudagraphs for the same inductor graph. Is it possible to reuse that here? Basically re-record if the address changes. This will hugely benefit `transformers` models where we could move `compile` to a smaller region and get almost as good as speedup as full model with very small compile time. ``` import torch # Make dynamo cache size 1 to fail on a recompilation torch._dynamo.config.recompile_limit = 1 @torch.compile(mode="reduce-overhead", fullgraph=True) def fn(x, static_y): return x + static_y x = torch.randn(4) static_y1 = torch.randn(4) static_y2 = torch.randn(4) torch._dynamo.mark_static_address(static_y1) torch._dynamo.mark_static_address(static_y2) fn(x, static_y1) fn(x, static_y2) ``` ### Error logs _No response_ ### Versions N/A cc @mcarilli @engineer_17 @engineer_02 @penguinwu @BoyuanFeng @chauhang

title: [pt2] [Precompile] Store parameters in BundledAOTAutogradCacheEntry

body: ### 🚀 The feature, motivation and pitch AOTDispatch has an extra wrapper here which adds flattened GraphModule parameters to the runtime arguments passed into the AOTAutograd callable. https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/2908c10259bac21b00e9b36318e364801e0ae910/torch/_functorch/aot_autograd.py#L1229 These need to be saved by BundledAOTAutogradCacheEntry or elsewhere for precompile. ### Alternatives _No response_ ### Additional context _No response_ cc @chauhang @penguinwu

title: compiler cache not work
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, compile-cache
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug In my program, I handle a batch of tasks, and I need to switch models dynamically based on task parameters. I’m trying to compile a specific branch of the forward function in my Flux model. The idea is to run an inference once, then save the compiled result, using the model path as the cache key. In practice, the compiled cache (in bytes) is successfully written to a file and can be loaded without errors. However, the inference time is almost the same as if no compilation had been used at all. Is there something I might be missing when saving/loading the compiled cache? Or does the compilation not take effect as expected? I have read this [doc about how to use mega-cache but not work as I thought](https://pytorch.ac.cn/tutorials/recipes/torch_compile_caching_tutorial.html#torch-compile-end-to-end-caching-mega-cache) my code run in multiprocessing load.py load model in every process. ```python def get_artifact_path(ckpt_path: str) -> Path: ckpt_hash = hashlib.sha256(ckpt_path.encode()).hexdigest() return ARTIFACT_CACHE_DIR / f"{ckpt_hash}.artifact" def save_cache_artifacts(ckpt_path): artifact_path = get_artifact_path(ckpt_path) if not artifact_path.exists(): artifacts = torch.compiler.save_cache_artifacts() if artifacts is not None: artifact_bytes, cache_info = artifacts if len(artifact_bytes) > 8: # ensure not empty artifact_path.write_bytes(artifact_bytes) print(f"[Cache] Saved compiler cache to {artifact_path}") return print("[Warning] No artifacts were saved!") def torch_compile(model, ckpt_path: str): artifact_path = get_artifact_path(ckpt_path) if artifact_path.exists(): print(f"[Cache] Loading compiler cache for {ckpt_path}") artifact_bytes = artifact_path.read_bytes() torch.compiler.load_cache_artifacts(artifact_bytes) else: # only speed up weave_speed_forward optimized_forward = torch.compile( getattr(model, "weave_speed_forward"), ) setattr(model, "weave_speed_forward", optimized_forward) def load_flow_model(ckpt_path: str, name: str, device: str | torch.device = "cuda", hf_download: bool = True): # Loading Flux print("Init model") # ckpt_path = configs[name].ckpt_path # if ( # ckpt_path is None # and configs[name].repo_id is not None # and configs[name].repo_flow is not None # and hf_download # ): # ckpt_path = hf_hub_download(configs[name].repo_id, configs[name].repo_flow) # Completely clear old model memory gc.collect() torch.cuda.empty_cache() with torch.device("meta" if ckpt_path is not None else device): model = Flux(configs[name].params).to(torch.bfloat16) if ckpt_path is not None: print("Loading checkpoint") # load_sft doesn't support torch.device directly # First load to CPU to avoid GPU memory spikes sd = load_sft(ckpt_path, device="cpu") # sd = load_sft(ckpt_path, device=str(device)) missing, unexpected = model.load_state_dict(sd, strict=False, assign=True) print_load_warning(missing, unexpected) # Then move to target device model = model.to(device) # Second memory cleanup to avoid crash during trace gc.collect() torch.cuda.empty_cache() # TODO: torch_compile torch_compile(model, ckpt_path) return model ``` inference.py inference in every process but save in main process like device 0 ```python .... get task parameters from input queue to swith model pipeline.load_flow_model(dit_ckpt_path) try: result = pipeline(prompt=prompt, width=width, height=height, ..... fast_forward=fast_forward, residual_diff_threshold=residual_diff_threshold, ) output_queue.put((seed, result)) # TODO: torch_compile if is_main: # save in main process util.save_cache_artifacts(ckpt_path=dit_ckpt_path) ``` model.py ``` class Flux(nn.Module): def forward( self, img: Tensor, ...... fast_forward: bool = True, residual_diff_threshold=0.10, ) -> Tensor: if fast_forward: return self.weave_speed_forward(img,........ residual_diff_threshold=residual_diff_threshold) ``` time record for inference in second : after few times/ switch from another model | | origin | torch.compile | compile cache (switch from Model A to B when B has cached before) | | ---------- | ------- | ------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ | | B_pipeline | 144/159 | 121/ 153 | 141/147 | | | | | | | | | | | I don't know why the third situation not the same as the second (torch.compile(the seconded time)) even slower just use compile without any cache ### Versions Collecting environment information... title: [dynamo && compiled autograd] compiled autograd recompiles when using custom autograd functions and compiling the forward with backend "eager"
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo, module: higher order operators, module: compiled autograd, module: pt2-dispatcher
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug In our current implementation, we're using torch.compile in the following way: ``` compiled_model = torch.compile(model, backend=custom_fwd_compiler) loss = compiled_model(*args, **kwargs) with torch._dynamo.compiled_autograd._enable( torch.compile( backend=custom_bwd_compiler, fullgraph=True, dynamic=True, ), dynamic=True, ): loss.backward() ``` We've encountered unexpected behavior where new graph traces are being generated unnecessarily. The captured graphs are nearly identical, with the only discernible difference being the line number references in the source code annotations **# File: <eval_with_key>.78:5 in forward, code: getitem = inputs[0]** and. **# File: <eval_with_key>.82:5 in forward, code: getitem = inputs[0]** as follows: Graph 0: ``` class GraphModule(torch.nn.Module): def forward(self, L_inputs_ : list, s40: "Sym(s40)", s1: "Sym(s1)", ... ... **# File: <eval_with_key>.78:5 in forward, code: getitem = inputs[0]** getitem: "bf16[s0, 1, s1]" = l_inputs_[0] getitem_1: "f64[]" = l_inputs_[1]; l_inputs_ = None ... ``` Graph 1: ``` class GraphModule(torch.nn.Module): def forward(self, L_inputs_ : list, s40: "Sym(s40)", s1: "Sym(s1)", ... ... **# File: <eval_with_key>.82:5 in forward, code: getitem = inputs[0]** getitem: "bf16[s0, 1, s1]" = l_inputs_[0] getitem_1: "f64[]" = l_inputs_[1]; l_inputs_ = None ... ``` It leads to significant time and memory overhead. Do you know how to prevent the unnecessary graph compile? ### Versions [pip3] numpy==1.23.3 [pip3] optree==0.14.0 [pip3] torch==2.6.0a0+git037c3cc [pip3] torchlibrosa==0.1.0 [pip3] torchvision==0.22.0a0+5f03dc5 [pip3] triton==3.0.0 [conda] Could not collect cc @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @chenyang78 @kadeng @amjames @zou3519 @ydwu4 @engineer_23 @bdhirsh

title: Segfault after clearing Dynamo Cache
labels: high priority, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo, compile-cache, dynamo-triage-jan2025
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug I came across this segfault while writing some tests, and @StrongerXi has reproduced: ```python import torch def random_tensor(size, dtype, **kwargs): if dtype in [torch.half, torch.bfloat16, torch.float, torch.double]: return torch.randn(size, dtype=dtype, **kwargs) elif dtype in [torch.uint8, torch.int8, torch.short, torch.int, torch.long]: return torch.randint(0, 100, size, dtype=dtype, **kwargs) else: raise ValueError("Unsupported data type") def cT(device, dtype): def T(*shape, requires_grad=False): return random_tensor( shape, requires_grad=requires_grad, device=device, dtype=dtype ) return T def pointwise_test_model(device, dtype, compile=True): T = cT(device, dtype) def model(): M = 256 N = 512 mat3 = T(M, N) mat4 = T(M, N) pointwise_output = torch.add(mat3, mat4).sin() return pointwise_output return model device = "cuda" dtype = torch.float16 om = pointwise_test_model(device, dtype, compile=False) print("compile 1") comp_omni = torch.compile(om) # print("run 1") # comp_omni() print("reset 1") torch._dynamo.reset() print("run 2") comp_omni() print("compile 2") comp_omni = torch.compile(om) print("run 3") comp_omni() ``` results in: ``` compile 1 reset 1 run 2 compile 2 run 3 Segmentation fault (core dumped) ``` I think disabling this line makes the segfault not trigger, so maybe something in that cache getting refcounted to zero and but then used? https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/5acb8d50801e6d110790993464611314dd1bd54b/torch/_dynamo/eval_frame.py#L276 ### Versions Collecting environment information...
